---
title: "Cameron & Trivedi -- Ch. 17: Count-data models (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Colin Cameron
- Pravin Trivedi
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
const DATA = let c = filter(d -> isdir(d) && (isfile(joinpath(d, "auto.dta")) || isdir(joinpath(d, "musr"))), ["../../data", "../data", "data"]); isempty(c) ? error("StatEcon example data not found; run this notebook from its own folder (examples/Cameron_Trivedi) or the repo root.") : first(c) end
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq
_rawval(x) = hasproperty(x, :value) ? x.value : x
```

The count-data estimators in this chapter now live in `StatEcon` and are
called directly below: the canonical models `stata_poisson`, `stata_nbreg`,
`stata_glm`, `stata_logit` (reused from earlier chapters) and the ch17
commands `stata_ztnb`, `stata_gnbreg`, `stata_zinb`, `stata_fmm_poisson`,
`stata_fmm_nbreg`, `stata_estat_lcmean`, `stata_poisson_gmm`,
`stata_estat_overid_gmm`, `stata_nl_exp`, `stata_boot_cf_poisson`,
`stata_margins_dydx_ztnb`, and `stata_estimates_stats`, plus the table
helpers `stata_summarize`, `stata_tabulate`, `stata_correlate`,
`stata_regress`. The pedagogical plotting / by-hand marginal-effect helpers
(`_c17_histogram_discrete_stata`, `_c17_histogram_grid_stata`,
`_c17_margins_dydx_at_binary_stata`, `_c17_margins_dydx_stata`) are kept
inline below as scaffolding.

```{julia}
# Pedagogical inline helpers (kept as scaffolding; `_c17_` prefixed).
# Discrete/grid count histograms, the by-hand delta-method marginal
# effects, and a dependency-free log-Γ used in the narrative demos.

# Dependency-free log-Γ (Lanczos g=7) for the by-hand count-probability
# demos (SpecialFunctions is not imported).
function _c17_loggamma(x::Real)
    g = 7.0
    c = (0.99999999999980993, 676.5203681218851, -1259.1392167224028,
         771.32342877765313, -176.61502916214059, 12.507343278686905,
         -0.13857109526572012, 9.9843695780195716e-6, 1.5056327351493116e-7)
    x < 0.5 && return log(π / sin(π * x)) - _c17_loggamma(1 - x)
    x -= 1; a = c[1]; t = x + g + 0.5
    for i in 1:8
        a += c[i + 1] / (x + i)
    end
    return 0.5 * log(2π) + (x + 0.5) * log(t) - t + log(a)
end

"""
    _c17_histogram_discrete_stata(x; xlabel, ylabel="freq", size=(600,400),
                              bins=nothing, title=nothing)

CairoMakie analogue of Stata's `histogram <var>, discrete xtitle("…")`.
Produces a one-panel `Figure` with integer-valued bins centred on each
observed count. Pass `bins = lo:1:hi` to override the auto-range
(useful for keeping multiple subplots on the same x-axis).
"""
function _c17_histogram_discrete_stata(x::AbstractVector;
                                   xlabel::AbstractString,
                                   ylabel::AbstractString = "freq",
                                   size::Tuple{Int,Int} = (600, 400),
                                   bins::Union{Nothing,AbstractRange} = nothing,
                                   title::Union{Nothing,AbstractString} = nothing)
    xint = Int.(x)
    rng  = bins === nothing ?
           ((minimum(xint) - 0.5):1:(maximum(xint) + 0.5)) : bins
    fig  = CairoMakie.Figure(; size = size)
    ax   = title === nothing ?
           CairoMakie.Axis(fig[1, 1]; xlabel, ylabel) :
           CairoMakie.Axis(fig[1, 1]; xlabel, ylabel, title)
    CairoMakie.hist!(ax, xint; bins = rng)
    return fig
end

"""
    _c17_histogram_grid_stata(panels; title="", ncols=2, bins=nothing,
                         xlabel="x", ylabel="freq", size=(900,700),
                         common=true, discrete=true, nbins=30)

CairoMakie analogue of Stata's `graph combine g1 g2 …, title(…)
[xcommon ycommon]`. `panels` is a vector of `(data, paneltitle)`
pairs; lays them out row-major in an `ncols`-wide grid with a shared
super-title. `common=true` links the axes across panels (Stata's
`xcommon ycommon`).

`discrete=true` (default) is for count data: each series is rounded to
`Int` and a single shared integer-centred `bins` range is used so the
bars align. `discrete=false` is for continuous series (e.g. fitted
means `predict, mu`): no rounding, and `nbins` equal-width bins span
the pooled data range (override with an explicit `bins`).
"""
function _c17_histogram_grid_stata(panels::AbstractVector;
                              title::AbstractString = "",
                              ncols::Int = 2,
                              bins::Union{Nothing,AbstractRange} = nothing,
                              xlabel::AbstractString = "x",
                              ylabel::AbstractString = "freq",
                              size::Tuple{Int,Int} = (900, 700),
                              common::Bool = true,
                              discrete::Bool = true,
                              nbins::Int = 30)
    if discrete
        series = [Int.(p[1]) for p in panels]
        rng = bins === nothing ?
              ((minimum(minimum, series) - 0.5):1:(maximum(maximum, series) + 0.5)) :
              bins
    else
        series = [Float64.(p[1]) for p in panels]
        if bins === nothing
            lo = minimum(minimum, series); hi = maximum(maximum, series)
            rng = range(lo, hi; length = nbins + 1)
        else
            rng = bins
        end
    end
    fig  = CairoMakie.Figure(; size = size)
    isempty(title) ||
        CairoMakie.Label(fig[0, 1:ncols], title; fontsize = 16,
                         font = :bold)
    axs = CairoMakie.Axis[]
    for (k, (_, ptitle)) in enumerate(panels)
        row = div(k - 1, ncols) + 1
        col = mod(k - 1, ncols) + 1
        ax  = CairoMakie.Axis(fig[row, col]; xlabel, ylabel, title = ptitle)
        CairoMakie.hist!(ax, series[k]; bins = rng)
        push!(axs, ax)
    end
    common && length(axs) > 1 && CairoMakie.linkaxes!(axs...)
    return fig
end

"""
    _c17_margins_dydx_at_binary_stata(β, V, c; specs, link=:logit,
        depvar="y", vce_label="OIM", level=0.95, n_obs=nothing,
        at_labels=nothing, noatlegend=false)

Stata-style `margins, dydx(*) at(...)` (MER) for a binary GLM (logit
or probit). Computes marginal effects at the user-specified point
`c` (length-k vector in β order, with intercept slot included), with
delta-method SEs via the analytic Jacobian.

`specs` is a vector of `(name::String, kind::Symbol, col_idx::Int)`:
- `kind = :continuous` → analytic derivative `ME = β_j · μ'(c'β)`
   - logit:  μ'(η) = Λ(1−Λ),    μ''(η) = Λ(1−Λ)(1−2Λ)
   - probit: μ'(η) = φ(η),      μ''(η) = −η · φ(η)
   Jacobian: e_j · μ'(η) + β_j · μ''(η) · c
- `kind = :factor` → discrete change `ME = μ(c | x_j=1) − μ(c | x_j=0)`
   Jacobian: μ'(η_1)·c_1 − μ'(η_0)·c_0  (where c_1/c_0 set x_j to 1/0)

Output mirrors Stata's `Conditional marginal effects` block for MER
(header / `dy/dx wrt:` / optional `At:` block + the variable rows).
Factor rows print as `1.<name>` per Stata's i.<var> labeling.

Returns `(; rows = Vector{NamedTuple}, c)` where each row has
`(name, me, se, z, p, ci_lo, ci_hi)`.
"""
function _c17_margins_dydx_at_binary_stata(β::AbstractVector, V::AbstractMatrix,
                                      c::AbstractVector;
                                      specs::AbstractVector,
                                      link::Symbol = :logit,
                                      depvar::AbstractString = "y",
                                      vce_label::AbstractString = "OIM",
                                      level::Float64 = 0.95,
                                      n_obs::Union{Nothing,Int} = nothing,
                                      at_labels::Union{Nothing,AbstractVector} = nothing,
                                      noatlegend::Bool = false,
                                      expression::Union{Nothing,AbstractString} = nothing)
    # Link evaluators: μ(η), μ'(η), μ''(η)
    if link == :logit
        μfn(η)   = 1 / (1 + exp(-η))
        μ1fn(η)  = (Λ = μfn(η); Λ * (1 - Λ))
        μ2fn(η)  = (Λ = μfn(η); Λ * (1 - Λ) * (1 - 2*Λ))
    elseif link == :probit
        μfn(η)   = Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), η)
        μ1fn(η)  = Distributions.pdf(Distributions.Normal(), η)
        μ2fn(η)  = -η * Distributions.pdf(Distributions.Normal(), η)
    else
        error("link=$link not supported (use :logit or :probit)")
    end

    crit = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Normal(), 1 - (1-level)/2)
    rows = NamedTuple[]
    for (name, kind, idx) in specs
        if kind == :continuous
            η  = LinearAlgebra.dot(c, β)
            μ1 = μ1fn(η);  μ2 = μ2fn(η)
            me = β[idx] * μ1
            J  = β[idx] .* μ2 .* c
            J[idx] += μ1
            row_label = name
        elseif kind == :factor
            c1 = copy(c); c1[idx] = 1.0
            c0 = copy(c); c0[idx] = 0.0
            η1 = LinearAlgebra.dot(c1, β); η0 = LinearAlgebra.dot(c0, β)
            me = μfn(η1) - μfn(η0)
            J  = μ1fn(η1) .* c1 .- μ1fn(η0) .* c0
            row_label = "1." * name
        else
            error("kind=$kind not supported (use :continuous or :factor)")
        end
        se = sqrt(LinearAlgebra.dot(J, V * J))
        z  = me / se
        p  = 2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), abs(z)))
        push!(rows, (name = row_label, me = me, se = se, z = z, p = p,
                     ci_lo = me - crit*se, ci_hi = me + crit*se))
    end

    # Stata `%9.0g` formatter
    function g9(x; w::Int=10, sig::Int=7)
        (ismissing(x) || !isfinite(x)) && return lpad(".", w)
        sig_use = sig
        s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
        cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 10 : 9
        while length(s) > cap && sig_use > 1
            sig_use -= 1
            s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
        end
        0 < abs(x) < 1 && (s = replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1."))
        lpad(s, w)
    end
    commafmt(num) = begin
        s = string(abs(num)); parts = String[]; i = length(s)
        while i >= 1; push!(parts, s[max(1, i-2):i]); i -= 3; end
        (num < 0 ? "-" : "") * join(reverse(parts), ",")
    end

    expr = expression === nothing ? "Pr($depvar), predict()" : expression
    println()
    # Header: "Conditional marginal effects" left-padded; "Number of obs"
    # right-aligned at col 78. Stata's exact widths: title in 57, label
    # "Number of obs" in 13, " = ", value right-aligned in 5 → total 78.
    if n_obs !== nothing
        Printf.@printf("%-57s%-13s = %5s\n",
                       "Conditional marginal effects",
                       "Number of obs", commafmt(n_obs))
    else
        println("Conditional marginal effects")
    end
    println("Model VCE: $vce_label")
    println()
    println("Expression: $expr")
    println("dy/dx wrt:  ", join([r.name for r in rows], " "))
    if !noatlegend && at_labels !== nothing
        for (i, kv) in enumerate(at_labels)
            prefix = i == 1 ? "At  : " : "      "
            Printf.@printf("%s%-12s = %s\n", prefix, string(kv.first),
                           string(kv.second))
        end
    end
    println()
    # Coefficient table: 12 (name) + 2 ( | ) + 11 (dy/dx) + 11 (se) +
    # 9 (z) + 8 (p) + 13 (ci_lo) + 12 (ci_hi) = 78 chars.
    println("-"^78)
    println("             |            Delta-method")
    Printf.@printf("%12s |      dy/dx   std. err.      z    P>|z|     [%g%% conf. interval]\n",
                   "", 100*level)
    println("-"^13, "+", "-"^64)
    for r in rows
        Printf.@printf("%12s |%s%s%9.2f%8.3f%s%s\n",
                       r.name,
                       g9(r.me;    w=11),
                       g9(r.se;    w=11),
                       r.z, r.p,
                       g9(r.ci_lo; w=13),
                       g9(r.ci_hi; w=12))
    end
    println("-"^78)
    # Stata adds this note when any factor variable appears in the dy/dx list.
    if any(s -> s[2] == :factor, specs)
        println("Note: dy/dx for factor levels is the discrete change from the base level.")
    end
    return (; rows, c)
end

"""
    _c17_margins_dydx_stata(β, V, X; factors, continuous,
                       atmean=false, at=nothing, at_labels=nothing,
                       noatlegend=false,
                       level=0.95, vce_label="Robust",
                       expression="Predicted number of events, predict()")

Unified Stata-style `margins, dydx(*)` — three modes via keyword args:

  - default (`atmean=false, at=nothing`) → **AME** (Stata: `margins, dydx(*)`).
    Title: "Average marginal effects". For each obs, compute ∂ŷ_i/∂x_j or the
    discrete change, then average over the sample.
  - `atmean=true` → **MEM** (Stata: `margins, dydx(*) atmean`). Title:
    "Conditional marginal effects". Evaluated at the sample-mean vector x̄.
  - `at=c` (length-k vector) → **MER** (Stata: `margins, dydx(*) at(...)`).
    Title: "Conditional marginal effects". Evaluated at the user-specified
    point c. Pass `at_labels = [:var=>v, ...]` for the "At:" block, or set
    `noatlegend=true` to suppress it (mirrors Stata's `noatlegend` option).

For each variable:
  - **Factor** (binary 0/1): discrete change ME = μ(x with j=1) − μ(x with j=0).
    Gradient: μ_1·x_1 − μ_0·x_0. For AME, this is averaged across observations.
  - **Continuous**: ME = β_j · μ(x). Gradient: e_j · μ + β_j · μ · x.

`factors` and `continuous` are vectors of `(name, col_idx::Int)` tuples giving
the variable name (no "1." prefix) and its column index in X.
"""
function _c17_margins_dydx_stata(β::AbstractVector, V::AbstractMatrix,
                            X::AbstractMatrix;
                            factors::AbstractVector,
                            continuous::AbstractVector,
                            kind::Symbol=:dydx,
                            atmean::Bool=false,
                            at::Union{Nothing,AbstractVector}=nothing,
                            at_labels::Union{Nothing,AbstractVector}=nothing,
                            noatlegend::Bool=false,
                            interactions::AbstractVector=NamedTuple[],
                            level::Float64=0.95,
                            vce_label::String="Robust",
                            expression::String="Predicted number of events, predict()")
    # `interactions` lets the helper handle product interaction columns
    # correctly. Each entry is a NamedTuple `(col=k, base=[i, j, ...])`
    # meaning column k of X is the elementwise product of columns
    # `base`. When a factor variable in `base` flips 0↔1 the helper
    # rebuilds col k from the (possibly modified) base columns; for a
    # continuous variable in `base`, the chain rule for ∂μ/∂x_j picks
    # up an extra `β_k · prod(other base cols)` term.
    # `kind` selects the column header AND the formula for *continuous*
    # variables (factor variables always use the discrete change, since
    # ey/ex of a 0/1 dummy is ill-defined). For Poisson with log link:
    #   :dydx → β_j · μ          (gradient e_j·μ + β_j·μ·x)
    #   :eyex → β_j · x_j        (gradient e_j·x_j;  ε doesn't depend on μ)
    #   :eydx → β_j              (gradient e_j;     semi-elasticity)
    #   :dyex → β_j · μ · x_j    (gradient depends on μ)
    kind in (:dydx, :eyex, :eydx, :dyex) ||
        error("kind must be :dydx, :eyex, :eydx, or :dyex; got $kind")
    kind_label = kind == :dydx ? "dy/dx" :
                 kind == :eyex ? "ey/ex" :
                 kind == :eydx ? "ey/dx" : "dy/ex"
    n, k = size(X)

    g9 = function(x; w_::Int=10, sig::Int=7)
        (ismissing(x) || !isfinite(x)) && return lpad(".", w_)
        sig_use = sig
        s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
        # Stata's %9.0g effective rule: cap pre-strip at 9 chars EXCEPT for
        # negative |x|<1 where cap=10 (the strip will save 1 char). Without
        # this, negative |x|<1 values lose a digit vs Stata's output.
        cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 10 : 9
        while length(s) > cap && sig_use > 1
            sig_use -= 1
            s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
        end
        0 < abs(x) < 1 && (s = replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1."))
        lpad(s, w_)
    end
    g_atval = function(x; sig::Int=7)
        sig_use = sig
        s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
        # Stata's %9.0g effective rule: cap pre-strip at 9 chars EXCEPT for
        # negative |x|<1 where cap=10 (the strip will save 1 char). Without
        # this, negative |x|<1 values lose a digit vs Stata's output.
        cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 10 : 9
        while length(s) > cap && sig_use > 1
            sig_use -= 1
            s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
        end
        0 < abs(x) < 1 && (s = replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1."))
        s
    end
    commafmt(num::Integer) = begin
        s = string(abs(num)); parts = String[]; i = length(s)
        while i >= 1; push!(parts, s[max(1, i-2):i]); i -= 3; end
        (num < 0 ? "-" : "") * join(reverse(parts), ",")
    end
    crit = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Normal(), 1 - (1-level)/2)
    _summary(dydx, se) = begin
        z = dydx / se
        p = 2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), abs(z)))
        (z, p, dydx - crit*se, dydx + crit*se)
    end

    # Determine evaluation mode
    if at !== nothing
        mode  = :atpoint
        x_eval = collect(at)
        length(x_eval) == k ||
            error("at must have length(β)=$k, got $(length(x_eval))")
    elseif atmean
        mode  = :atmean
        x_eval = vec(Statistics.mean(X, dims = 1))
    else
        mode  = :average
        x_eval = nothing
    end

    title = mode == :average ? "Average marginal effects" :
                               "Conditional marginal effects"

    # _refresh_interactions! rebuilds any interaction column whose `base`
    # includes `idx` from the (possibly mutated) base columns. Used when
    # flipping a factor between 0 and 1 in the AME / atmean / atpoint path.
    function _refresh_interactions!(Xm::AbstractMatrix, idx::Integer)
        for intr in interactions
            if idx in intr.base
                col = ones(size(Xm, 1))
                for b in intr.base
                    col .*= Xm[:, b]
                end
                Xm[:, intr.col] .= col
            end
        end
    end
    function _refresh_interactions_vec!(xv::AbstractVector, idx::Integer)
        for intr in interactions
            if idx in intr.base
                v = 1.0
                for b in intr.base
                    v *= xv[b]
                end
                xv[intr.col] = v
            end
        end
    end

    # Compute each variable's dy/dx and its delta-method SE
    rows = Tuple[]
    for (nm, idx) in factors
        if mode == :average
            X0 = copy(X); X0[:, idx] .= 0.0; _refresh_interactions!(X0, idx)
            X1 = copy(X); X1[:, idx] .= 1.0; _refresh_interactions!(X1, idx)
            μ0 = exp.(X0 * β); μ1 = exp.(X1 * β)
            dydx = Statistics.mean(μ1 .- μ0)
            gvec = vec((μ1' * X1 .- μ0' * X0) ./ n)
        else
            x0 = copy(x_eval); x0[idx] = 0.0; _refresh_interactions_vec!(x0, idx)
            x1 = copy(x_eval); x1[idx] = 1.0; _refresh_interactions_vec!(x1, idx)
            μ0 = exp(LinearAlgebra.dot(x0, β))
            μ1 = exp(LinearAlgebra.dot(x1, β))
            dydx = μ1 - μ0
            gvec = μ1 .* x1 .- μ0 .* x0
        end
        se = sqrt(gvec' * V * gvec)
        push!(rows, ("1.$nm", dydx, se, _summary(dydx, se)...))
    end
    # Interactions involving variable `idx` — used by :dydx for both the AME
    # slope and its gradient (chain rule picks up β_intr · prod_other terms).
    function _intr_extras(idx::Integer, X_::AbstractMatrix)
        out = NamedTuple[]
        for intr in interactions
            if idx in intr.base
                po = ones(size(X_, 1))
                for b in intr.base
                    b == idx && continue
                    po .*= X_[:, b]
                end
                push!(out, (col = intr.col, prod_other = po))
            end
        end
        out
    end
    function _intr_extras_vec(idx::Integer, xv::AbstractVector)
        out = NamedTuple[]
        for intr in interactions
            if idx in intr.base
                v = 1.0
                for b in intr.base
                    b == idx && continue
                    v *= xv[b]
                end
                push!(out, (col = intr.col, prod_other_at = v))
            end
        end
        out
    end

    for (nm, idx) in continuous
        if mode == :average
            μ_all = exp.(X * β)
            μ̄    = Statistics.mean(μ_all)
            x̄_j  = Statistics.mean(X[:, idx])
            if kind == :dydx
                # ∂μ_i/∂x_j = μ_i · slope_i where slope_i = β_j + Σ β_intr · prod_other
                xtras = _intr_extras(idx, X)
                slope = fill(β[idx], n)
                for x in xtras
                    slope .+= β[x.col] .* x.prod_other
                end
                dydx = Statistics.mean(μ_all .* slope)
                gvec = vec((μ_all .* slope)' * X) ./ n
                gvec[idx] += sum(μ_all) / n
                for x in xtras
                    gvec[x.col] += sum(μ_all .* x.prod_other) / n
                end
            elseif kind == :eyex
                # ey/ex averaged over the sample = β_j · mean(x_j)  (Poisson log)
                dydx = β[idx] * x̄_j
                gvec = zeros(k); gvec[idx] = x̄_j
            elseif kind == :eydx
                dydx = β[idx]
                gvec = zeros(k); gvec[idx] = 1.0
            else  # :dyex — dy/d(ln x) = β_j · μ · x_j
                dydx = β[idx] * μ̄ * x̄_j
                gvec = zeros(k); gvec[idx] = μ̄ * x̄_j
                gvec .+= β[idx] * x̄_j .* vec((μ_all' * X) ./ n)
            end
        else
            μ_at = exp(LinearAlgebra.dot(x_eval, β))
            xj   = x_eval[idx]
            if kind == :dydx
                # slope_at = β_j + Σ β_intr · prod_other (eval at point)
                xtras = _intr_extras_vec(idx, x_eval)
                slope_at = β[idx]
                for x in xtras
                    slope_at += β[x.col] * x.prod_other_at
                end
                dydx = μ_at * slope_at
                gvec = μ_at * slope_at .* x_eval
                gvec[idx] += μ_at
                for x in xtras
                    gvec[x.col] += μ_at * x.prod_other_at
                end
            elseif kind == :eyex
                # ey/ex at the eval point = β_j · x_j  (Poisson log link makes
                # this independent of μ — gradient picks up only the β_j slot)
                dydx = β[idx] * xj
                gvec = zeros(k); gvec[idx] = xj
            elseif kind == :eydx
                dydx = β[idx]
                gvec = zeros(k); gvec[idx] = 1.0
            else  # :dyex
                dydx = β[idx] * μ_at * xj
                gvec = zeros(k); gvec[idx] = μ_at * xj
                gvec .+= β[idx] * μ_at * xj .* x_eval
            end
        end
        se = sqrt(gvec' * V * gvec)
        push!(rows, (string(nm), dydx, se, _summary(dydx, se)...))
    end

    dydx_names = vcat(["1.$nm" for (nm, _) in factors],
                      [string(nm) for (nm, _) in continuous])

    println()
    Printf.@printf("%-57s%-13s = %5s\n",
                   title, "Number of obs", commafmt(n))
    Printf.@printf("Model VCE: %s\n", vce_label)
    println()
    Printf.@printf("Expression: %s\n", expression)
    Printf.@printf("%s wrt:  %s\n", kind_label, join(dydx_names, " "))

    # "At:" block (only for atmean / atpoint, and only if not suppressed)
    if !noatlegend && mode != :average
        if mode == :atmean
            at_lines = Tuple[]
            for (nm, idx) in factors
                m1 = Statistics.mean(X[:, idx])
                push!(at_lines, ("0.$nm", 1.0 - m1))
                push!(at_lines, ("1.$nm", m1))
            end
            for (nm, idx) in continuous
                push!(at_lines, (string(nm), x_eval[idx]))
            end
            max_name_w = maximum(length(t[1]) for t in at_lines)
            for (i, (nm, val)) in enumerate(at_lines)
                prefix = i == 1 ? "At: " : "    "
                Printf.@printf("%s%-*s = %s (mean)\n",
                               prefix, max_name_w, nm, g_atval(val))
            end
        elseif at_labels !== nothing && !isempty(at_labels)
            max_name_w = maximum(length(string(p_.first)) for p_ in at_labels)
            val_strs   = [Printf.@sprintf("%g", float(p_.second)) for p_ in at_labels]
            max_val_w  = maximum(length, val_strs)
            for (i, p_) in enumerate(at_labels)
                prefix = i == 1 ? "At: " : "    "
                Printf.@printf("%s%-*s = %s\n",
                               prefix, max_name_w, string(p_.first),
                               lpad(val_strs[i], max_val_w))
            end
        end
    end

    println()
    println("-"^78)
    println("             |            Delta-method")
    Printf.@printf("             |      %s   std. err.      z    P>|z|     [95%% conf. interval]\n", kind_label)
    println("-"^13, "+", "-"^64)
    for (lab, dydx, se, z, p, lo, hi) in rows
        Printf.@printf("%12s | %s  %s  %s   %s    %s  %s\n",
                       lab, g9(dydx; w_=10), g9(se; w_=9),
                       Printf.@sprintf("%7.2f", z),
                       Printf.@sprintf("%.3f", p),
                       g9(lo; w_=9), g9(hi; w_=10))
    end
    println("-"^78)
    !isempty(factors) && println("Note: dy/dx for factor levels is the discrete change from the base level.")
    return rows
end
```

# 17 Count-data models

## 17.1 Introduction

## 17.2 Features of count data

### 17.2.1 Generated Poisson data

```{julia}
#=
* Poisson (mu=1) generated data
quietly set obs 10000
set seed 10101              // set the seed !
generate xpois= rpoisson(1) // draw from Poisson(mu=1)
summarize xpois
tabulate xpois
* Persist Stata's draw so the Julia translation can read the same
* realised sample (Stata and Julia have different RNG streams).
export delimited xpois using "musr/xpois.csv", replace
=#
#=
* Poisson (mu=1) generated data
quietly set obs 10000
set seed 10101              // set the seed !
generate xpois= rpoisson(1) // draw from Poisson(mu=1)
summarize xpois
tabulate xpois
* Persist Stata's draw so Julia can read the same realised sample.
export delimited xpois using "musr/xpois.csv", replace
=#

# Stata's `rpoisson` and Julia's `Distributions.Poisson` use
# different RNG streams, so re-drawing in Julia under the same seed
# would not reproduce Stata's sample. The Stata chunk persists its
# draw to `musr/xpois.csv`; we read that file here so the sample
# moments and frequency table match Stata exactly.
df_pois = CSV.read("musr/xpois.csv", DataFrames.DataFrame)
xpois   = Int.(df_pois.xpois)

# `summarize xpois`.
stata_summarize(df_pois, [:xpois])

# `tabulate xpois`.
stata_tabulate(df_pois, :xpois);
```

```{julia}
#=
*histogram xpois, discrete xtitle("Poisson")
*graph save musr/mus17xp, replace
=#
#=
histogram xpois, discrete xtitle("Poisson")
graph save musr/mus17xp, replace
=#

# Discrete-count histogram via `_c17_histogram_discrete_stata` (chunk 1)
# — CairoMakie wrapper for Stata's `histogram <var>, discrete`.
# Stata's `graph save` step has no Julia analogue; the figure is
# returned inline by Quarto.
_c17_histogram_discrete_stata(xpois; xlabel = "Poisson")
```

### 17.2.2 Overdispersion and negative binomial data

```{julia}
#=
* Negative binomial (mu=1 var=2) generated data
set seed 10101                   // set the seed !
generate xg = rgamma(1,1)
generate xnegbin = rpoisson(xg)  // NB generated as a Poisson-gamma mixture
* Persist Stata's draw so the Julia translation can read the same
* realised sample (Stata and Julia have different RNG streams).
export delimited xnegbin using "musr/xnegbin.csv", replace

summarize xnegbin
tabulate xnegbin
=#
#=
* Negative binomial (mu=1 var=2) generated data
set seed 10101                   // set the seed !
generate xg = rgamma(1,1)
generate xnegbin = rpoisson(xg)  // NB generated as a Poisson-gamma mixture
summarize xnegbin
tabulate xnegbin
* Persist Stata's draw so the Julia translation can read the same
* realised sample (Stata and Julia have different RNG streams).
export delimited xnegbin using "musr/xnegbin.csv", replace
=#

# Negative binomial as a Poisson-gamma mixture: x_i ~ Poisson(g_i),
# g_i ~ Gamma(1, 1). Marginal E[x] = 1, Var[x] = 1 + 1/k = 2 with
# k = 1 (Stata's `rgamma(α, β)` is shape-scale parameterisation).
#
# Read Stata's realised draw from `musr/xnegbin.csv` rather than
# re-drawing in Julia (the two RNG streams differ, so re-drawing
# would not reproduce Stata's sample moments and tabulation).
df_negbin = CSV.read("musr/xnegbin.csv", DataFrames.DataFrame)
xnegbin   = Int.(df_negbin.xnegbin)

# `summarize xnegbin`.
stata_summarize(df_negbin, [:xnegbin])

# `tabulate xnegbin`.
stata_tabulate(df_negbin, :xnegbin);
```

```{julia}
#=
*histogram xnegbin, discrete xtitle("Negative binomial")
*graph save musr/mus17negbin, replace
=#
#=
histogram xnegbin, discrete xtitle("Negative binomial")
graph save musr/mus17negbin, replace
=#

# Discrete-count histogram via `_c17_histogram_discrete_stata` (chunk 1)
# Stata's `graph save` step has no Julia analogue; the figure is
# returned inline by Quarto.
_c17_histogram_discrete_stata(xnegbin; xlabel = "Negative binomial")

```

### 17.2.3 Modeling strategies

### 17.2.4 Estimation methods

## 17.3 Empirical example 1

### 17.3.1 Data summary

```{julia}
#=
drop _all 

* Summary statistics for doctor visits data
capture use musr/mus17data.dta  
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Trying alternative path..."
    use mus17data.dta
}
global xlist private medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr 
summarize docvis $xlist
=#
#=
drop _all

* Summary statistics for doctor visits data
capture use musr/mus17data.dta
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Trying alternative path..."
    use mus17data.dta
}
global xlist private medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr
summarize docvis $xlist
=#

# Load `mus17data.dta` — Medical Expenditure Panel Survey (MEPS)
# subsample, dependent variable `docvis` (# doctor visits, count).
# Promote any Stata Float32 columns to Float64 so downstream MLE /
# GLM helpers don't trip on a mixed-precision matrix.
df17 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus17data.dta"))
for c in DataFrames.names(df17)
    if eltype(df17[!, c]) <: Union{Missing, Float32}
        df17[!, c] = convert.(Union{Missing, Float64}, df17[!, c])
    end
end

# Stata's `global xlist …` → Julia `Vector{Symbol}`.
xlist17 = [:private, :medicaid, :age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr]

# `summarize docvis $xlist`.
stata_summarize(df17, vcat([:docvis], xlist17));
```

```{julia}
#=
* Tabulate docvis after recoding values > 10 to ranges 11-40 or 41-143
generate dvrange = docvis
recode dvrange (11/40 = 40) (41/143 = 143)
tabulate dvrange
=#
#=
* Tabulate docvis after recoding values > 10 to ranges 11-40 or 41-143
generate dvrange = docvis
recode dvrange (11/40 = 40) (41/143 = 143)
tabulate dvrange
=#

# `recode` collapses the long right tail of `docvis` into two
# bins so the frequency table fits on the page: any value in
# [11, 40] is rounded up to 40 (interpreted as "11–40"), and any
# value in [41, 143] to 143 ("41–143"). Values ≤ 10 keep their
# original integer.
df17.dvrange = [v ≤ 10 ? Int(v) :
                v ≤ 40 ? 40   :
                          143
                for v in Int.(_rawval.(df17.docvis))]

# `tabulate dvrange`.
stata_tabulate(df17, :dvrange);
```

### 17.3.2 Poisson model

#### Poisson model results

```{julia}
#=
* Poisson with default ML standard errors
poisson docvis $xlist, nolog
=#
#=
* Poisson with default ML standard errors
poisson docvis $xlist, nolog
=#

# Default-ML Poisson regression: docvis ~ private + medicaid + age +
# age2 + educyr + actlim + totchr (log link). `stata_poisson`
# (chunk 1) with `vce = :oim` reproduces Stata's standard `poisson
# … , nolog` output — Poisson regression header, LR chi2(k) /
# Prob > chi2 / Pseudo R² block, "Log likelihood = …", and the
# coefficient table with Stata's %9.0g formatting.
df17c  = DataFrames.dropmissing(df17[:, vcat([:docvis], xlist17)])
fml17  = GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + age2 +
                                educyr + actlim + totchr)

pois17_fit = stata_poisson(df17c, fml17; vce = :oim)

# Keep a GLM.jl-backed handle in `pois17` for downstream chunks that
# call GLM.predict / GLM.coefnames / etc. (`stata_poisson` returns
# β / V / X / coefnames already, but a few later chunks reach into
# the GLM model object directly).
pois17 = GLM.glm(fml17, df17c, Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink());
```

```{julia}
#=
* Squared correlation between y and yhat
drop yphat
predict yphat, n
quietly correlate docvis yphat
display "Squared correlation between y and yhat = " r(rho)^2
=#
#=
* Squared correlation between y and yhat
drop yphat
predict yphat, n
quietly correlate docvis yphat
display "Squared correlation between y and yhat = " r(rho)^2
=#

# Stata's `predict yphat, n` after `poisson` gives ŷ = exp(x'β̂) —
# the conditional mean μ̂_i — which is GLM.predict(pois17) here.
# `correlate docvis yphat` returns `r(rho)` = corr(y, ŷ); squaring
# it gives the count-data analogue of R² (the "explained-variation"
# measure used in Cameron & Trivedi §17.3).
yphat = GLM.predict(pois17)
Printf.@printf("Squared correlation between y and yhat = %.7f\n",
               Statistics.cor(df17c.docvis, yphat)^2)
```

#### Robust estimate of VCE for Poisson MLE

```{julia}
#=
/*
* Poisson with robust standard errors
display "xlist = $xlist"      // confirm it's still the 7 regressors
correlate $xlist              // any pair with |ρ|≈1?
count if missing(docvis) | missing(private) | missing(medicaid) ///
       | missing(age) | missing(age2) | missing(educyr)         ///
       | missing(actlim) | missing(totchr)


poisson docvis $xlist, vce(robust) nolog  // Poisson robust SEs
*/
quietly poisson docvis $xlist               // <- no vce(robust)
predict muhat, n
quietly generate ystar = ((docvis-muhat)^2 - docvis)/muhat
regress ystar muhat, noconstant noheader

=#
#=
* Poisson with robust standard errors
poisson docvis $xlist, vce(robust) nolog
*// Poisson robust SEs
=#

# `stata_poisson` with `vce = :robust` (chunk 1) — same MLE
# point estimates as the default-ML fit, but the variance is the
# Huber–White sandwich (X'WX)⁻¹ X'diag((y−μ̂)²) X (X'WX)⁻¹ with the
# n/(n−1) finite-sample correction Stata applies. Header switches
# "LR chi²" → "Wald chi²" and "Log likelihood" → "Log
# pseudolikelihood"; the table picks up the "Robust" subheader and
# lowercase "std. err." label, mirroring Stata's display.
pois17_robust = stata_poisson(df17c, fml17; vce = :robust);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Poisson with robust standard errors
display "xlist = $xlist"      // confirm it's still the 7 regressors
correlate $xlist              // any pair with |ρ|≈1?
count if missing(docvis) | missing(private) | missing(medicaid) ///
       | missing(age) | missing(age2) | missing(educyr)         ///
       | missing(actlim) | missing(totchr)


poisson docvis $xlist, vce(robust) nolog  // Poisson robust SEs

quietly poisson docvis $xlist               // <- no vce(robust)
predict muhat, n
quietly generate ystar = ((docvis-muhat)^2 - docvis)/muhat
regress ystar muhat, noconstant noheader
=#

# Sanity-check the regressor set before fitting `poisson … ,
# vce(robust)` — in Stata this chunk threw r(3301) ("subscript
# invalid" inside Mata's `rob__compute()`) because `age` and
# `age2` were correlated at ρ ≈ 0.9992, making the sandwich
# H⁻¹·X'diag((y−μ̂)²)X·H⁻¹ numerically rank-deficient. Julia's
# linear solver (Cholesky on Symmetric) handles the same matrix
# without complaint, so we run the diagnostics for parity and
# then both the robust-SE fit and the overdispersion test.
println("xlist = ", join(string.(xlist17), " "))                # `display "xlist = $xlist"`
stata_correlate(df17c, xlist17)                                 # `correlate $xlist`
n_miss = sum(any(ismissing, eachrow(df17[!, vcat([:docvis], xlist17)])))
Printf.@printf("count if missing(...) = %d\n", n_miss)

# `poisson docvis $xlist, vce(robust) nolog` — Huber–White sandwich.
stata_poisson(df17c, fml17; vce = :robust)

# `quietly poisson docvis $xlist` — default-ML refit (quiet) for the
# overdispersion test; β̂ and μ̂ are identical to the robust fit.
fit_q17 = stata_poisson(df17c, fml17; vce = :oim, quiet = true)
df17c.muhat = GLM.predict(pois17)
df17c.ystar = ((df17c.docvis .- df17c.muhat).^2 .- df17c.docvis) ./
              df17c.muhat

# `regress ystar muhat, noconstant noheader` — auxiliary OLS via
# `FixedEffectModels.reg` + chunk-1 `stata_regress(model;
# noheader = true)`.
m_over_17 = FixedEffectModels.reg(df17c,
                                  GLM.@formula(ystar ~ muhat + 0))
stata_regress(m_over_17);
```

#### Coefficient interpretation and marginal effects

```{julia}
#=
* Average marginal effect for Poisson
quietly poisson docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr
*, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*)
=#
#=
* Average marginal effect for Poisson
quietly poisson docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*)
=#

# `margins, dydx(*)` after `poisson … , vce(robust)` — Stata's
# Average Marginal Effect block. With `i.private i.medicaid i.actlim`
# declared as factors, Stata reports a **discrete change** for those
# three (μ̂|x=1 − μ̂|x=0 averaged over the sample, rows labelled
# `1.<var>`) and the **derivative** β_j·μ̂ averaged over the sample
# for the four continuous regressors (age, age2, educyr, totchr).
#
# `_c17_margins_dydx_stata` (chunk 1) takes (β, V, X) in Stata-display
# order and a (`factors`, `continuous`) split with their column
# indices into the design matrix. `pois17_robust` from the previous
# chunk supplies all three.
_c17_margins_dydx_stata(pois17_robust.β, pois17_robust.V, pois17_robust.X;
    factors    = [(:private, 1), (:medicaid, 2), (:actlim, 6)],
    continuous = [(:age, 3), (:age2, 4), (:educyr, 5), (:totchr, 7)],
    kind       = :dydx,
    vce_label  = "Robust");

```

```{julia}
#=
#=
// Following not included in book
* Poisson as glm
glm docvis $xlist, family(poisson) link(log) robust  // glm
=#

=#
#=
// Following not included in book
* Poisson as glm
glm docvis $xlist, family(poisson) link(log) robust  // glm
=#

# Stata's `glm … , family(poisson) link(log) robust` — same MLE
# (β̂) as `poisson … , vce(robust)`, but printed under the GLM
# header (Generalized linear models / Deviance / Pearson / Variance
# function / Link function / AIC / BIC) and with the "Robust
# std. err." subheader on the coefficient block. `stata_glm`
# (chunk 1) reproduces this full layout.
stata_glm(df17c, fml17;
          family = :poisson,
          link   = :log,
          vce    = :robust);
```

### 17.3.3 NB2 model

#### NB2 model results

```{julia}
#=
* Standard negative binomial (NB2) with default SEs
nbreg docvis $xlist, nolog
=#
#=
* Standard negative binomial (NB2) with default SEs
nbreg docvis $xlist, nolog
=#

# NB2: V(y|x) = μ + α·μ². `stata_nbreg` (chunk 1) fits jointly by
# MLE on (β, lnα), prints Stata's `nbreg … , nolog` block (Negative
# binomial regression header, LR chi²(k) / Prob / Pseudo R², the
# Stata-style coefficient table, plus the `/lnalpha` row, the back-
# transformed `alpha` row, and the boundary-LR test "LR test of
# alpha=0: chibar²(01)" against the Poisson restriction α = 0).
stata_nbreg(df17c, fml17; vce = :oim)

# Bind a GLM-backed handle for downstream chunks that reach into the
# NB model directly (e.g. `nb17.model.rr.d.r` for the NB dispersion,
# or `GLM.predict(nb17)` for the conditional mean).
nb17 = GLM.negbin(fml17, df17c, GLM.LogLink());
```

```{julia}
#=
* Squared correlation between y and yhat
predict ynbhat, n
quietly correlate docvis ynbhat
display "Squared correlation between y and yhat = " r(rho)^2
=#
#=
* Squared correlation between y and yhat
predict ynbhat, n
quietly correlate docvis ynbhat
display "Squared correlation between y and yhat = " r(rho)^2
=#

# `predict ynbhat, n` after `nbreg` gives ŷ = exp(x'β̂) — the NB2
# conditional mean μ̂ (same closed form as Poisson, since NB2 and
# Poisson share the mean function under the log link). The squared
# Pearson correlation between y and ŷ is the count-data analogue
# of R² that Cameron & Trivedi report.
ynbhat = GLM.predict(nb17)
Printf.@printf("Squared correlation between y and yhat = %.7f\n",
               Statistics.cor(df17c.docvis, ynbhat)^2)
```

#### Fitted probabilities for Poisson and NB2 models

#### Manual countfit

```{julia}
#=
* Poisson: Sample vs avg predicted probabilities of y = 0, 1, ..., 5
* countfit is deprecated - use manual calculation of predicted probabilities
quietly poisson docvis $xlist
predict phat, n
forvalues i = 0/5 {
    quietly count if docvis == `i'
    local actual`i' = r(N) / _N
    quietly generate double prob`i' = exp(-phat) * phat^`i' / exp(lnfactorial(`i'))
    quietly summarize prob`i'
    local pred`i' = r(mean)
    display "Count `i': Actual = " %6.4f `actual`i'' "  Predicted = " %6.4f `pred`i''
}
drop phat prob*
=#

# Manual `countfit` — for each k ∈ {0, …, 5}, compare the empirical
# frequency P̂(y = k) to the mean predicted Poisson probability
# (1/N) Σ_i exp(−μ̂_i)·μ̂_i^k / k!. Under a correctly-specified
# Poisson the two columns should agree; gaps signal lack of fit
# (typically excess zeros and a too-thin upper tail).
phat17 = GLM.predict(pois17)
println("Poisson: Sample vs avg predicted probabilities of y = 0, ..., 5")
for k in 0:5
    actual = Statistics.mean(df17c.docvis .== k)
    pred   = Statistics.mean(exp.(.-phat17) .* phat17.^k ./ factorial(k))
    Printf.@printf("Count %d: Actual = %6.4f  Predicted = %6.4f\n",
                   k, actual, pred)
end
```

```{julia}
#=
* NB2: Sample vs average predicted probabilities of y = 0, 1, ..., 5
* countfit is deprecated - use manual calculation for negative binomial
quietly nbreg docvis $xlist
predict phat, n
local alpha = e(alpha)
forvalues i = 0/5 {
    quietly count if docvis == `i'
    local actual`i' = r(N) / _N
    quietly generate double prob`i' = exp(lngamma(`i' + 1/`alpha') - lngamma(`i' + 1) - lngamma(1/`alpha')) * (1/(1 + `alpha'*phat))^(1/`alpha') * (`alpha'*phat/(1 + `alpha'*phat))^`i'
    quietly summarize prob`i'
    local pred`i' = r(mean)
    display "Count `i': Actual = " %6.4f `actual`i'' "  Predicted = " %6.4f `pred`i''
}
drop phat prob*
=#

# Manual `countfit` for NB2. Under NB2 with mean μ and dispersion α,
#   P(Y = k | x) = Γ(k + 1/α) / (Γ(k+1)·Γ(1/α)) ·
#                  (1/(1+α·μ))^(1/α) · (α·μ/(1+α·μ))^k
# Same actual-vs-mean-predicted comparison as the Poisson chunk;
# under NB2 the extra mass at k = 0 (from the gamma mixing) closes
# most of the Poisson gap there.
phat17_nb = GLM.predict(nb17)
α17_nb    = 1 / nb17.model.rr.d.r      # GLM's `r` parameter holds 1/α
println("NB2: Sample vs avg predicted probabilities of y = 0, ..., 5")
for k in 0:5
    actual = Statistics.mean(df17c.docvis .== k)
    p_k    = exp.(_c17_loggamma(k + 1/α17_nb) -
                  _c17_loggamma(k + 1) -
                  _c17_loggamma(1/α17_nb)) .*
             (1 ./ (1 .+ α17_nb .* phat17_nb)).^(1/α17_nb) .*
             (α17_nb .* phat17_nb ./
              (1 .+ α17_nb .* phat17_nb)).^k
    pred = Statistics.mean(p_k)
    Printf.@printf("Count %d: Actual = %6.4f  Predicted = %6.4f\n",
                   k, actual, pred)
end
```

#### prvalue equivalent

```{julia}
#=
* NB2: Predicted NB2 probabilities at x = x* of y = 0, 1, ..., 5
* prvalue is deprecated - manually calculate predicted probabilities
quietly nbreg docvis $xlist
local alpha = e(alpha)
predict xb, xb
quietly replace xb = exp(xb) if !missing(xb)
forvalues i = 0/5 {
    quietly generate double prob`i' = exp(lngamma(`i' + 1/`alpha') - lngamma(`i' + 1) - lngamma(1/`alpha')) * (1/(1 + `alpha'*xb))^(1/`alpha') * (`alpha'*xb/(1 + `alpha'*xb))^`i' if private==1 & medicaid==1
    quietly summarize prob`i' if private==1 & medicaid==1
    display "Pr(y=`i' | private=1, medicaid=1) = " %6.4f r(mean)
    drop prob`i'
}
drop xb
=#

# Stata's `prvalue` analogue: average the predicted NB2 probability
# at each k over the subsample with `private == 1 & medicaid == 1`
# (representative "covered both ways" patient). Same NB2 closed
# form as the manual-countfit chunk above; restricting μ̂ to the
# subsample is the Julia counterpart of Stata's `if` qualifier.
sub17       = DataFrames.filter(r -> r.private == 1 && r.medicaid == 1, df17c)
phat17_sub  = GLM.predict(nb17, sub17)
α17_nb      = 1 / nb17.model.rr.d.r

println("NB2 P(y = k | private = 1, medicaid = 1):")
for k in 0:5
    p_k = exp.(_c17_loggamma(k + 1/α17_nb) -
               _c17_loggamma(k + 1) -
               _c17_loggamma(1/α17_nb)) .*
          (1 ./ (1 .+ α17_nb .* phat17_sub)).^(1/α17_nb) .*
          (α17_nb .* phat17_sub ./
           (1 .+ α17_nb .* phat17_sub)).^k
    Printf.@printf("Pr(y = %d | private = 1, medicaid = 1) = %6.4f\n",
                   k, Statistics.mean(p_k))
end
```

#### Discussion

#### Generalized NB model

```{julia}
#=
* Generalized negative binomial with alpha parameterized
capture noisily gnbreg docvis $xlist, lnalpha(female bh) nolog
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Note: gnbreg convergence failed"
}
=#
#=
* Generalized negative binomial with alpha parameterized
capture noisily gnbreg docvis $xlist, lnalpha(female bh) nolog
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Note: gnbreg convergence failed"
}
=#

# `stata_gnbreg` (chunk 1) — Generalized NB2 with the dispersion
# parameter modelled as ln(α_i) = γ_0 + γ_female·female + γ_bh·bh.
# Jointly MLE in (β, γ) by LBFGS + ForwardDiff with OIM SEs from a
# finite-difference Hessian. Prints the two-equation Stata block
# (`docvis` outcome panel and `lnalpha` variance panel) plus the
# Wald χ²(k_slopes) and Pseudo R² banner.
#
# Wrapped in try/catch as the Julia counterpart of Stata's
# `capture noisily` — `gnbreg` is notorious for failing to
# converge on real data (Cameron & Trivedi flag this), and we'd
# rather print a note than crash the notebook.
try
    # Pass the full `df17` (not `df17c`) — `df17c` was narrowed to
    # `[docvis; xlist17]` and no longer contains `female` / `bh`.
    # The helper drops missings on whatever it needs internally.
    global gnb17 = stata_gnbreg(df17,
        GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + age2 +
                              educyr + actlim + totchr);
        lnalpha_vars = [:female, :bh])
catch err
    println("Note: gnbreg convergence failed — $(err)")
end
```

### 17.3.4 Nonlinear least-squares estimation

```{julia}
#=
* Nonlinear least squares
capture noisily nl (docvis = exp({xb: $xlist one})), vce(robust) nolog
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Note: nl convergence failed"
}
=#
#=
* Nonlinear least squares
capture noisily nl (docvis = exp({xb: $xlist one})), vce(robust) nolog
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Note: nl convergence failed"
}
=#

# `stata_nl_exp` (chunk 1) — Stata's `nl (docvis = exp({xb: …})),
# vce(robust)`. NLS with exponential mean μ = exp(x'β), Huber–White
# sandwich SEs. Same β identification as Poisson MLE (since the
# Poisson score is also a moment of `y − exp(x'β)`); coefficients
# should match Poisson coefficients up to numerical optimisation
# noise, but the NLS objective ignores the Poisson variance and is
# generally less efficient.
#
# Wrapped in try/catch as Stata's `capture noisily` analogue —
# the NLS surface for exponential means is shallow and the
# textbook flags non-convergence.
try
    global nl17 = stata_nl_exp(df17c, :docvis, xlist17; vce = :robust)
catch err
    println("Note: nl convergence failed — $(err)")
end
```

### 17.3.5 Hurdle model

```{julia}
#=
* Hurdle: Pr(y=0)=pi and Pr(y=k)=(1-pi) x Poisson(2) truncated at 0
quietly set obs 10000
set seed 10101             // set the seed !
scalar pi=1-(1-exp(-2))/2  // Probability y=0
generate xhurdle = 0
scalar minx = 0
while minx == 0 {
 generate xph = rpoisson(2)
 quietly replace xhurdle = xph if xhurdle==0
 drop xph
 quietly summarize xhurdle
 scalar minx = r(min)
}
replace xhurdle = 0 if runiform() < pi
summarize xhurdle
* Persist Stata's draw so the Julia translation can read the same
* realised sample (Stata and Julia have different RNG streams).

export delimited xhurdle using "musr/xhurdle.csv", replace
=#
#=
* Hurdle: Pr(y=0)=pi and Pr(y=k)=(1-pi) x Poisson(2) truncated at 0
quietly set obs 10000
set seed 10101             // set the seed !
scalar pi=1-(1-exp(-2))/2  // Probability y=0
generate xhurdle = 0
scalar minx = 0
while minx == 0 {
 generate xph = rpoisson(2)
 quietly replace xhurdle = xph if xhurdle==0
 drop xph
 quietly summarize xhurdle
 scalar minx = r(min)
}
replace xhurdle = 0 if runiform() < pi
summarize xhurdle
=#

# Stata's two-stage hurdle DGP (rejection-sample ZT-Poisson(2),
# then knock back to zero with probability π) uses Stata's RNG
# stream; re-drawing in Julia would not reproduce the moments.
# Read Stata's persisted realisation from `musr/xhurdle.csv` so
# `stata_summarize` matches Stata exactly.
df_hurdle = CSV.read("musr/xhurdle.csv", DataFrames.DataFrame)
xhurdle   = Int.(df_hurdle.xhurdle)

# `summarize xhurdle`.
stata_summarize(df_hurdle, [:xhurdle]);
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
*histogram xhurdle, discrete xtitle("Hurdle Poisson")
*graph save musr/mus17hurdle, replace 
=#
#=
// Following not included in book
histogram xhurdle, discrete xtitle("Hurdle Poisson")
graph save musr/mus17hurdle, replace
=#

# `_c17_histogram_discrete_stata` (chunk 1) — CairoMakie wrapper for
# Stata's `histogram <var>, discrete`. The bimodal shape (spike at
# 0 from the hurdle mass, then the Poisson(2) bump over positives)
# is what motivates the two-part model in this section.
_c17_histogram_discrete_stata(xhurdle; xlabel = "Hurdle Poisson")
```

#### Variants of the hurdle model

#### Application of the hurdle model

```{julia}
#=
* Hurdle logit-nb model manually
logit docvis $xlist, nolog
=#
#=
* Hurdle logit-nb model manually
logit docvis $xlist, nolog
=#

# Hurdle part 1 — the "any visit?" participation equation. Stata's
# `logit docvis $xlist` treats `docvis` as binary (0 vs any nonzero
# value), so we build the indicator `docpos = 1{docvis > 0}` first
# and fit the logit on it. `stata_logit` (chunk 1) prints Stata's
# `Logistic regression` block (LR chi²/Prob/Pseudo R² header plus
# the coefficient table). Part 2 (the zero-truncated NB on the
# positives) was fit earlier in this section.
df17c.docpos = Int.(df17c.docvis .> 0)
stata_logit(df17c,
    GLM.@formula(docpos ~ private + medicaid + age + age2 +
                          educyr + actlim + totchr); vce = :oim);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Second step uses positives only
summarize docvis if docvis > 0
=#
#=
* Second step uses positives only
summarize docvis if docvis > 0
=#

# Hurdle part 2 is fit on the positive subsample only. `summarize
# docvis if docvis > 0` checks the conditional distribution of the
# count given participation — `DataFrames.filter` is the Julia
# counterpart of Stata's `if docvis > 0` qualifier.
stata_summarize(DataFrames.filter(:docvis => >(0), df17c), [:docvis]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Zero-truncated negative binomial 
ztnb docvis $xlist if docvis>0, nolog 
=#
#=
* Zero-truncated negative binomial
ztnb docvis $xlist if docvis>0, nolog
=#

# Hurdle part 2 — `stata_ztnb` (chunk 1) fits the zero-truncated NB2
# on the positive subsample (Stata's `if docvis>0`). The density of
# each positive count is renormalised by 1 − P(Y=0), so the model
# describes the count *given* at least one visit. `DataFrames.filter`
# supplies the `if docvis>0` estimation sample; the helper does not
# filter on its own.
stata_ztnb(DataFrames.filter(:docvis => >(0), df17c),
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + age2 +
                          educyr + actlim + totchr); vce = :oim);
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* hnblogit command is not available - hurdle model already estimated above manually
* Using logit for hurdle and nbreg for positive counts
display "Note: hnblogit command not available, use manual hurdle estimation shown above"
=#
#=
* margins for marginal effects of first part
quietly logit docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) atmean noatlegend
=#

# Marginal effects of the hurdle participation (part 1) logit, at the
# sample means (Stata's `margins, dydx(*) atmean`). `i.private`,
# `i.medicaid`, `i.actlim` are factors → discrete change
# Λ(η̄|x=1) − Λ(η̄|x=0); the rest are continuous → β_j·Λ(η̄)(1−Λ(η̄)).
#
# Re-fit the participation logit silently with robust SEs
# (`stata_logit`, chunk 1), then evaluate `_c17_margins_dydx_at_binary_stata`
# (chunk 1) at the column means `c̄` with a (name, kind, idx) spec
# list. `noatlegend = true` suppresses the "At:" block, matching
# Stata's `noatlegend`.
df17c.docpos = Int.(df17c.docvis .> 0)
logit_mfx = stata_logit(df17c,
    GLM.@formula(docpos ~ private + medicaid + age + age2 +
                          educyr + actlim + totchr);
    vce = :robust, quiet = true)

c̄_mfx = vec(Statistics.mean(logit_mfx.X, dims = 1))
_c17_margins_dydx_at_binary_stata(logit_mfx.β, logit_mfx.V, c̄_mfx;
    specs = [("private",  :factor,     1),
             ("medicaid", :factor,     2),
             ("age",      :continuous, 3),
             ("age2",     :continuous, 4),
             ("educyr",   :continuous, 5),
             ("actlim",   :factor,     6),
             ("totchr",   :continuous, 7)],
    link = :logit, depvar = "docpos", vce_label = "Robust",
    noatlegend = true, n_obs = logit_mfx.n);
```

```{julia}
#=
* margins for marginal effects of first part
quietly logit docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) atmean noatlegend
=#
#=
* margins for marginal effects of second part
quietly ztnb docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr if docvis>0, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) atmean noatlegend
=#

# Marginal effects of the hurdle count (part 2) ZT-NB on its
# conditional mean m(x) = μ/(1 − P(Y=0)), at the sample means.
# Re-fit the ZT-NB silently on the positives with robust SEs, then
# `stata_margins_dydx_ztnb` (chunk 1) computes dy/dx at the means:
# factors (i.private/medicaid/actlim) get the discrete change, the
# rest get the derivative; delta-method SEs use a finite-difference
# gradient against the robust vcov.
#
# `stata_ztnb` keeps β in GLM order (column 1 = intercept), so the
# regressor column indices in `specs` are 2..8.
ztnb_mfx = stata_ztnb(DataFrames.filter(:docvis => >(0), df17c),
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + age2 +
                          educyr + actlim + totchr);
    vce = :robust, quiet = true)

stata_margins_dydx_ztnb(ztnb_mfx;
    specs = [("private",  :factor,     2),
             ("medicaid", :factor,     3),
             ("age",      :continuous, 4),
             ("age2",     :continuous, 5),
             ("educyr",   :continuous, 6),
             ("actlim",   :factor,     7),
             ("totchr",   :continuous, 8)],
    atmean = true, depvar = "docvis", vce_label = "Robust",
    noatlegend = true);
```

```{julia}
#=
* margins for marginal effects of second part
quietly ztnb docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr if docvis>0, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) atmean noatlegend
=#
#=
// Following repeats earlier code as xpois and xnegbin have since been dropped
* Poisson (mu=1) and NB(1,1) generated data
quietly set obs 10000
set seed 10101                   // set the seed !
generate xpois= rpoisson(1)
generate xg = rgamma(1,1)
generate xnegbin = rpoisson(xg) // NB draws
=#

# Re-create the Poisson(1) and NB(1,1) reference samples for the
# four-panel comparison figure (the earlier `xpois`/`xnegbin` were
# dropped). Drawn fresh in Julia rather than read from the §17.2
# CSVs — note this `xnegbin` is NOT the §17.2.2 realisation: there
# `xg = rgamma(1,1)` was the first draw after the seed, whereas here
# `xpois` consumes part of the stream first, so the gamma mixing
# draws land at a different position. As these only feed a shape-
# comparison plot and a mean/sd printout, the Monte-Carlo-noise
# difference from Stata is immaterial.
# N17 = 10,000 simulated obs (rebind here — the original §17.3.5
# binding was removed when that chunk switched to reading xhurdle
# from CSV; `xpmix` below also relies on it).
N17 = 10_000
Random.seed!(10101)
xpois2   = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Poisson(1), N17)
xg2      = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Gamma(1, 1), N17)
xnegbin2 = [Distributions.rand(Distributions.Poisson(g)) for g in xg2]
@printf("xpois2:   mean = %.4f, sd = %.4f\n",
        Statistics.mean(xpois2), Statistics.std(xpois2))
@printf("xnegbin2: mean = %.4f, sd = %.4f\n",
        Statistics.mean(xnegbin2), Statistics.std(xnegbin2))
```

#### Simulated FMM sample with comparisons

```{julia}
#=
* Mixture: Poisson(.5) with prob .9 and Poisson(5.5) with prob .1
set seed 10101        // set the seed !
generate xp1= rpoisson(.5)
generate xp2= rpoisson(5.5)
summarize xp1 xp2
generate xpmix = xp1                     // (was: rename xp1 xpmix) keep xp1 for export
quietly replace xpmix = xp2 if runiform() > 0.9
summarize xpmix
* Persist all three columns so the Julia translation reproduces both
* `summarize` blocks exactly (xpmix needs the runiform() selection).
export delimited xp1 xp2 xpmix using "musr/xpmix.csv", replace
=#
#=
* Mixture: Poisson(.5) with prob .9 and Poisson(5.5) with prob .1
set seed 10101        // set the seed !
generate xp1= rpoisson(.5)
generate xp2= rpoisson(5.5)
summarize xp1 xp2
rename xp1 xpmix
quietly replace xpmix = xp2 if runiform() > 0.9
summarize xpmix
=#

# Two-component Poisson mixture: 0.9·Poisson(0.5) + 0.1·Poisson(5.5),
# so E[x] = 0.9·0.5 + 0.1·5.5 = 1.0 (same mean as the Poisson(1) and
# NB(1,1) references) but with a heavier right tail. The mixture
# assignment uses Stata's `runiform() > 0.9` draw, which Julia can't
# reproduce, so we read Stata's realised xp1/xp2/xpmix from
# `musr/xpmix.csv` (persisted by the Stata chunk) — both summaries
# then match Stata exactly.
df_mix = CSV.read("musr/xpmix.csv", DataFrames.DataFrame)
xp1   = Int.(df_mix.xp1)
xp2   = Int.(df_mix.xp2)
xpmix = Int.(df_mix.xpmix)

# `summarize xp1 xp2`.
stata_summarize(df_mix, [:xp1, :xp2])

# `summarize xpmix`.
stata_summarize(df_mix, [:xpmix]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Tabulate the mixture data
tabulate xpmix
=#
#=
* Tabulate the mixture data
tabulate xpmix
=#

# `tabulate xpmix` — frequency table of the mixture counts.
stata_tabulate(DataFrames.DataFrame(xpmix = xpmix), :xpmix);
```

```{julia}
#=
/*
// Following not included in book
histogram xpmix, discrete xtitle("Finite mixture Poisson")
graph save musr/mus17pmix, replace
*/
=#
#=
// Following not included in book
histogram xpmix, discrete xtitle("Finite mixture Poisson")
graph save musr/mus17pmix, replace
=#

# `_c17_histogram_discrete_stata` (chunk 1) — discrete-count histogram of
# the finite-mixture sample. The long right shoulder from the 10%
# Poisson(5.5) component is the visible signature of the mixture.
_c17_histogram_discrete_stata(xpmix; xlabel = "Finite mixture Poisson")
```

```{julia}
#=
/*
* Compare the four distributions, all with mean 1
graph combine musr/mus17xp.gph musr/mus17negbin.gph musr/mus17pmix.gph ///
musr/mus17hurdle.gph, title("Four different distributions with mean = 1") ///
ycommon xcommon
*/
=#
#=
* Compare the four distributions, all with mean 1
graph combine musr/mus17xp.gph musr/mus17negbin.gph musr/mus17pmix.gph ///
musr/mus17hurdle.gph, title("Four different distributions with mean = 1") ///
ycommon xcommon
=#

# `_c17_histogram_grid_stata` (chunk 1) — CairoMakie analogue of Stata's
# `graph combine … , ycommon xcommon`. Lays the four mean-1
# distributions in a 2×2 grid with linked (common) axes, so the
# differing tail behaviour at a shared scale is directly comparable:
# Poisson(1) is tight, NB(1,1) is overdispersed, the finite mixture
# has a secondary bump near 5, and the hurdle piles mass at 0.
_c17_histogram_grid_stata(
    [(xpois2,   "Poisson(1)"),
     (xnegbin2, "Negative binomial"),
     (xpmix,    "Finite mixture Poisson"),
     (xhurdle,  "Hurdle Poisson")];
    title = "Four different distributions with mean = 1",
    bins  = -0.5:1:15.5)
```

#### ML estimation of the FMM

#### The fmm equivalent

#### Application: Poisson finite-mixture model

```{julia}
#=
* Compare the four distributions, all with mean 1
summarize xpois xnegbin xpmix xhurdle
=#
#=
* Compare the four distributions, all with mean 1
summarize xpois xnegbin xpmix xhurdle
=#

# `summarize xpois xnegbin xpmix xhurdle` — all four are constructed
# to have population mean 1, but differ in spread: Poisson(1) has
# variance 1, the others are overdispersed (var ≈ 2 for NB and the
# mixture, larger still for the hurdle). Stata's `xpois`/`xnegbin`
# are this section's `xpois2`/`xnegbin2`.
stata_summarize(
    DataFrames.DataFrame(xpois   = xpois2,   xnegbin = xnegbin2,
                         xpmix   = xpmix,    xhurdle = xhurdle),
    [:xpois, :xnegbin, :xpmix, :xhurdle]);
```

#### Interpretation

```{julia}
#=
* Finite-mixture model using fmm command with constant probabilities
use musr/mus17data.dta, clear
fmm 2, vce(robust): poisson docvis $xlist
=#
#=
* Finite-mixture model using fmm command with constant probabilities
use musr/mus17data.dta, clear
fmm 2, vce(robust): poisson docvis $xlist
=#

# `stata_fmm_poisson` (chunk 1) — C-component Poisson finite mixture
# with constant class probabilities, Stata's `fmm 2, vce(robust):
# poisson docvis $xlist`. Prints the latent-class logit block plus a
# per-component Poisson coefficient block; returns the per-component
# β / μ / class shares. (The mixture density is accumulated with
# log-sum-exp — `exp(logpmf)` underflows for the long docvis tail,
# max = 144, which would otherwise drop the high-count obs that pin
# down the high-mean component. Components are sorted by ascending
# mean so the labeling matches Stata's class 1 = lower mean.)
#
# Stata's session had `$xlist` empty, so the command ran intercept-
# only `fmm 2: poisson docvis` (only `_cons` per class). We match with
# `docvis ~ 1`: class 1 _cons = 1.2111 (μ̂₁ = 3.357), class 2 _cons =
# 2.6986 (μ̂₂ = 14.86), 2.Class _cons = −0.8411 (π̂₁ = 0.699),
# log-pseudolikelihood −12434.33.
fmm_res = stata_fmm_poisson(df17c, GLM.@formula(docvis ~ 1);
                            ncomponents = 2, vce = :robust)

# Bind the names the downstream chunks expect.
β_fmm1, β_fmm2 = fmm_res.β_components
μ_fmm1, μ_fmm2 = fmm_res.μ_components
π_fmm1         = fmm_res.π[1]
cn_fmm         = fmm_res.coefnames
β_pois_start   = fmm_res.β_pois_start     # shared with the NB-FMM section
y_fmm          = fmm_res.y
X_fmm          = fmm_res.X
k_fmm          = fmm_res.k;
```

```{julia}
#=
* Predict y for two components
quietly fmm 2, vce(robust): poisson docvis $xlist
estat lcmean
predict yfit*, mu
summarize yfit*
=#
#=
* Predict y for two components
quietly fmm 2, vce(robust): poisson docvis $xlist
estat lcmean
predict yfit*, mu
summarize yfit*
=#

# `estat lcmean` — latent-class marginal means from the FMM fit
# (`fmm_res`, bound above). `stata_estat_lcmean` (chunk 1) prints the
# per-class average predicted mean with delta-method SEs. Under the
# intercept-only fit these are just the component means μ̂₁ ≈ 3.357
# (low utilisers) and μ̂₂ ≈ 14.86 (high utilisers).
stata_estat_lcmean(fmm_res; depvar = "docvis")

# `predict yfit*, mu` — per-component fitted means μ̂_c. Intercept-only
# ⇒ each column is constant across observations.
df17c.yfit1 = fmm_res.μ_components[1]
df17c.yfit2 = fmm_res.μ_components[2]

# `summarize yfit*`.
stata_summarize(df17c, [:yfit1, :yfit2]);
```

#### Comparing marginal effects

```{julia}
#=
* Marginal effects for component 1
margins, dydx(*) predict(mu class(1)) atmean noatlegend
* Marginal effects for component 2 
margins, dydx(*) predict(mu class(2)) atmean noatlegend
=#
#=
* Marginal effects for component 1
margins, dydx(*) predict(mu class(1)) atmean noatlegend
* Marginal effects for component 2
margins, dydx(*) predict(mu class(2)) atmean noatlegend
=#

# `margins, dydx(*) predict(mu class(c))` differentiates the component
# mean μ_c = exp(x'β_c) w.r.t. the regressors. With the intercept-only
# fit (`$xlist` empty in Stata's session) there ARE no regressors to
# differentiate, so — like Stata, which returns "nothing to compute"
# for `dydx(*)` after an intercept-only model — there is no marginal-
# effects table here.
#
# (When `$xlist` is defined and the FMM is fit with `fml17`, each
# component's dy/dx is a plain Poisson-mean `margins, dydx(*) atmean`
# on β_c, computable via `_c17_margins_dydx_stata(fmm_res.β_components[c],
# fmm_res.V[(c-1)k+1:ck, …], fmm_res.X; factors=…, continuous=…)`.)
if fmm_res.k <= 1
    println("margins, dydx(*): no covariates in the intercept-only " *
            "mixture — nothing to differentiate.")
else
    k = fmm_res.k
    specs_f = [(:private, 2), (:medicaid, 3), (:actlim, 7)]
    specs_c = [(:age, 4), (:age2, 5), (:educyr, 6), (:totchr, 8)]
    for c in 1:2
        rng  = ((c-1)*k + 1):(c*k)
        V_cc = fmm_res.V[rng, rng]
        println("\n=== Component $c: margins, dydx(*) predict(mu class($c)) atmean ===")
        _c17_margins_dydx_stata(fmm_res.β_components[c], V_cc, fmm_res.X;
            factors = specs_f, continuous = specs_c,
            kind = :dydx, atmean = true, noatlegend = true,
            vce_label = "Robust")
    end
end
```

```{julia}
#=
set scheme s1mono  /* Graphics scheme */
* Create histograms of fitted values  
quietly histogram yfit1, name(_comp_1, replace)
quietly histogram yfit2, name(_comp_2, replace)
quietly graph combine _comp_1 _comp_2
=#
#=
set scheme s1mono  /* Graphics scheme */
* Create histograms of fitted values
quietly histogram yfit1, name(_comp_1, replace)
quietly histogram yfit2, name(_comp_2, replace)
quietly graph combine _comp_1 _comp_2
=#

# Side-by-side histograms of the two components' fitted means
# (`predict, mu class(c)`) via `_c17_histogram_grid_stata` (chunk 1) with
# `discrete = false`. Under the intercept-only fit each component's
# μ̂_c is constant, so each panel is a single spike at μ̂₁ ≈ 3.36 and
# μ̂₂ ≈ 14.86 — the figure becomes meaningful (a spread) only with the
# full-covariate mixture.
_c17_histogram_grid_stata(
    [(df17c.yfit1, "Component 1 (π̂ = $(round(fmm_res.π[1], digits=3)))"),
     (df17c.yfit2, "Component 2 (π̂ = $(round(fmm_res.π[2], digits=3)))")];
    discrete = false, nbins = 30, xlabel = "fitted μ")
```

#### Application: NB finite-mixture model

```{julia}
#=
* 2-component mixture of NB1 
fmm 2, vce(robust): nbreg docvis $xlist, dispersion(mean)
=#
#=
* 2-component mixture of NB1
fmm 2, vce(robust): nbreg docvis $xlist, dispersion(mean)
=#

# `stata_fmm_nbreg` (chunk 1) — 2-component NB2 finite mixture, each
# component with its own dispersion α_c. Stata's `dispersion(mean)`
# *is* NB2 (Var = μ + α·μ²); the "NB1" in the comment is a textbook
# labelling slip. Prints the full Stata `fmm … : nbreg` layout (class
# logit block + per-class `_cons` and `/docvis lnalpha` rows with
# robust SEs), components sorted by ascending mean.
#
# Intercept-only to match Stata's empty-`$xlist` session: class 1
# _cons = 1.9085 (μ̂₁ = 6.743), lnα₁ = −0.4853 (α̂₁ = 0.615); class 2
# _cons = 1.9931 (μ̂₂ = 7.338), lnα₂ = 1.1220 (α̂₂ = 3.071); 2.Class
# _cons = −1.8679 (π̂₁ = 0.866); log-pseudolikelihood −10948.71.
# Note the mixture separates by DISPERSION (α 0.62 vs 3.07), not mean
# (6.7 vs 7.3) — the high-α class captures the heavy-tailed users.
nbfmm_res = stata_fmm_nbreg(df17c, GLM.@formula(docvis ~ 1);
                            ncomponents = 2);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Fitted values for 2-component NB1 mixture
capture drop yfit1 yfit2 
predict yfit*, mu
summarize yfit*
=#
#=
* Fitted values for 2-component NB1 mixture
capture drop yfit1 yfit2
predict yfit*, mu
summarize yfit*
=#

# `predict yfit*, mu` after the NB FMM — per-component fitted means
# μ̂_c (from `nbfmm_res`, bound above). `capture drop yfit1 yfit2`
# clears the Poisson-FMM yfit columns first; here we just overwrite
# them with the NB-mixture values. Intercept-only ⇒ each column is
# constant (μ̂₁ ≈ 6.74, μ̂₂ ≈ 7.34) — the NB mixture separates the
# population by dispersion, not mean, so the two fitted means are
# close.
df17c.yfit1 = nbfmm_res.μ_components[1]
df17c.yfit2 = nbfmm_res.μ_components[2]

# `summarize yfit*`.
stata_summarize(df17c, [:yfit1, :yfit2]);
```

#### Model selection

#### Cautionary note

## 17.4 Empirical example 2

### 17.4.1 Zero-inflated data

```{julia}
#=
*drop _all

*set more off
use musr/mus17data_z.dta 
* Summary stats for ER use model
use musr/mus17data_z.dta      
global xlist1 age actlim totchr 
summarize er $xlist1
tabulate er 
=#
#=
*drop _all

*set more off
use musr/mus17data_z.dta
* Summary stats for ER use model
use musr/mus17data_z.dta
global xlist1 age actlim totchr
summarize er $xlist1
tabulate er
=#

# Load `mus17data_z.dta` — emergency-room visits (`er`), a sharply
# zero-inflated count (most people have zero ER visits). The
# regressor set is `$xlist1` = age + actlim + totchr. Promote Stata
# Float32 columns to Float64 for the downstream MLE helpers.
df17z = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus17data_z.dta"))
for c in DataFrames.names(df17z)
    if eltype(df17z[!, c]) <: Union{Missing, Float32}
        df17z[!, c] = convert.(Union{Missing, Float64}, df17z[!, c])
    end
end
xlist17z = [:age, :actlim, :totchr]

# `summarize er $xlist1`.
stata_summarize(df17z, vcat([:er], xlist17z))

# `tabulate er` — frequency table showing the large mass at zero.
stata_tabulate(df17z, :er);
```

### 17.4.2 Models for zero-inflated data

### 17.4.3 NB2 model results

```{julia}
#=
* NB2 for er
nbreg er $xlist1, nolog
=#

# NB2 for ER visits — `stata_nbreg` (chunk 1) prints Stata's `nbreg
# er age actlim totchr, nolog` block (header, coefficient table,
# /lnalpha + alpha rows, LR test of α = 0). The large α here flags
# heavy overdispersion driven by the excess zeros — motivating the
# zero-inflated models in the chunks that follow.
df17zc = DataFrames.dropmissing(df17z[:, vcat([:er], xlist17z)])
fml17z = GLM.@formula(er ~ age + actlim + totchr)

stata_nbreg(df17zc, fml17z; vce = :oim)

# GLM-backed handle for the downstream `prcounts` predicted-probability
# chunk (`nb17z.model.rr.d.r` for the dispersion, `GLM.predict(nb17z)`
# for μ̂).
nb17z = GLM.negbin(fml17z, df17zc, GLM.LogLink());
```

#### prcounts equivalent

```{julia}
#=
* Sample average fitted probabilities of y = 0 to max()
* prcounts is deprecated - calculate manually using predict
predict xb, xb
local alpha = e(alpha)
forvalues i = 0/3 {
    generate erpr`i' = exp(lngamma(`i' + 1/`alpha') - lngamma(`i' + 1) - lngamma(1/`alpha')) * (1/(1 + `alpha'*exp(xb)))^(1/`alpha') * (`alpha'*exp(xb)/(1 + `alpha'*exp(xb)))^`i'
}
drop xb
summarize erpr*
=#

# `prcounts` analogue: for each count j ∈ {0,…,3}, the fitted NB2
# probability per observation
#   erpr_j(x_i) = Γ(j+1/α)/(Γ(j+1)Γ(1/α)) ·
#                 (1/(1+α·μ̂_i))^{1/α} · (α·μ̂_i/(1+α·μ̂_i))^j,
# μ̂_i = exp(x_i'β̂). `summarize erpr*` then reports each column's
# sample mean = the average fitted P(er = j). Comparing these to the
# empirical frequencies (the `tabulate er` above) exposes the NB2's
# under-prediction of zeros — the motivation for the ZINB below.
α_z = 1 / nb17z.model.rr.d.r
μ_z = GLM.predict(nb17z)
for j in 0:3
    gj = exp(_c17_loggamma(j + 1/α_z) -
             _c17_loggamma(j + 1) -
             _c17_loggamma(1/α_z))
    df17zc[!, Symbol("erpr$j")] = gj .*
        (1 ./ (1 .+ α_z .* μ_z)).^(1/α_z) .*
        (α_z .* μ_z ./ (1 .+ α_z .* μ_z)).^j
end

# `summarize erpr*`.
stata_summarize(df17zc, [:erpr0, :erpr1, :erpr2, :erpr3]);
```

### 17.4.4 ZINB results

```{julia}
#=
* Zero-inflated negative binomial for er
zinb er $xlist1, inflate($xlist1) forcevuong nolog
=#

# `stata_zinb` (chunk 1) — zero-inflated NB2: an NB2 count equation
# (er ~ age + actlim + totchr) mixed with a logit "always-zero"
# inflation on the same `$xlist1`. Prints Stata's `zinb` block (count
# eq, inflate eq, /lnalpha + alpha) and the `forcevuong` Vuong test of
# ZINB vs plain NB2 (z > 0 favours ZINB). Default OIM SEs match
# Stata's `zinb` without `vce(robust)`.
zinb_res = stata_zinb(df17zc, GLM.@formula(er ~ age + actlim + totchr);
                      inflate_vars = [:age, :actlim, :totchr],
                      vce = :oim, vuong = true);
```

### 17.4.5 Model comparison

#### Model comparison via AIC/BIC

```{julia}
#=
* Comparison of NB and ZINB using manual probability calculations
* countfit is deprecated - estimate both models and compare fit manually
quietly nbreg er $xlist1
estimates store NB
quietly zinb er $xlist1, inflate($xlist1)
estimates store ZINB
* Compare AIC and BIC
estimates stats NB ZINB
=#

# `estimates stats NB ZINB` — `stata_estimates_stats` (chunk 1) builds
# the AIC/BIC comparison table. Degrees of freedom: NB2 has 4 count
# coefficients + lnα = 5; ZINB adds the 4-parameter inflation logit →
# 9. Lower AIC/BIC favours the ZINB, the formal counterpart of the
# Vuong test above.
#
# `ll(null)` is each command's `e(ll_0)`: for NB it's the intercept-
# only NB2; for ZINB it's the count equation reduced to its intercept
# with the inflation equation kept (NOT intercept-only everywhere,
# which would collapse to the NB null). `stata_zinb` returns that as
# `ll_null`.
let
    nb_ll   = GLM.loglikelihood(nb17z)
    nb_df   = length(GLM.coef(nb17z)) + 1            # + lnalpha
    nb_ll0  = GLM.loglikelihood(GLM.negbin(GLM.@formula(er ~ 1),
                                           df17zc, GLM.LogLink()))
    zinb_df = length(zinb_res.β) + length(zinb_res.γ) + 1
    stata_estimates_stats(
        [("NB",   DataFrames.nrow(df17zc), nb_ll,       nb_df,   nb_ll0),
         ("ZINB", zinb_res.n,              zinb_res.ll, zinb_df, zinb_res.ll_null)])
end;
```

## 17.5 Models with endogenous regressors

### 17.5.1 Structural-model approach (control function)

#### Model and assumptions

#### Two-step estimation

#### Application

```{julia}
#=
clear all
* First-stage linear regression
use "C:\EC\BSE\DSDM\Term 2\24DM021_Causal Inference and Machine Learning\References\Cameron & Trivedi\musr\mus17data.dta", clear
global xlist2 medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr
regress private $xlist2 income ssiratio, vce(robust)
predict lpuhat, residual
=#
#=
clear all
* First-stage linear regression
use musr/mus17data.dta
global xlist2 medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr
regress private $xlist2 income ssiratio, vce(robust)
predict lpuhat, residual
=#

# Control-function (two-step) approach to the endogenous regressor
# `private`. First stage: OLS of `private` on the exogenous `$xlist2`
# plus the excluded instruments `income` and `ssiratio`, robust SEs.
# The first-stage residual `lpuhat` is the control function added to
# the second-stage Poisson (next subsection). `$xlist2` here drops
# `private` from the §17.3 `$xlist` (it's the endogenous variable).
xlist17_2 = [:medicaid, :age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr]
df17_iv = DataFrames.dropmissing(
    df17[:, vcat([:docvis, :private, :income, :ssiratio], xlist17_2)])

m_first17 = FixedEffectModels.reg(df17_iv,
    GLM.@formula(private ~ medicaid + age + age2 + educyr + actlim +
                            totchr + income + ssiratio),
    FixedEffectModels.Vcov.robust())

# `regress … , vce(robust)` — Stata-style table via `stata_regress`
# (chunk 1).
stata_regress(m_first17)

# `predict lpuhat, residual` — first-stage residual (control function).
df17_iv.lpuhat = df17_iv.private .-
                 FixedEffectModels.predict(m_first17, df17_iv);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Second-stage Poisson with robust SEs
poisson docvis private $xlist2 lpuhat, vce(robust) nolog
=#
#=
* Second-stage Poisson with robust SEs
poisson docvis private $xlist2 lpuhat, vce(robust) nolog
=#

# Control-function second stage: Poisson of docvis on the endogenous
# `private`, the exogenous `$xlist2`, and the first-stage residual
# `lpuhat` (from §17.5.1). A significant `lpuhat` coefficient is the
# endogeneity test — if `private` were exogenous its control function
# would carry no information. `stata_poisson` (chunk 1) with
# `vce = :robust`. (Note: these robust SEs do NOT correct for the
# first-stage estimation of `lpuhat`; a proper two-step / bootstrap
# vcov is wider — the textbook uses this fit for the endogeneity
# test, not final inference.)
stata_poisson(df17_iv,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + age2 + educyr +
                          actlim + totchr + lpuhat); vce = :robust);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Program and bootstrap for Poisson two-step estimator
program endogtwostep, eclass
  version 11
  tempname b
  capture drop lpuhat2
  regress private $xlist2 income ssiratio
  predict lpuhat2, residual
  poisson docvis private $xlist2 lpuhat2
  matrix `b' = e(b)
  ereturn post `b'
end
bootstrap _b, reps(400) seed(10101) nodots nowarn: endogtwostep
=#
#=
* Program and bootstrap for Poisson two-step estimator
program endogtwostep, eclass
  version 11
  tempname b
  capture drop lpuhat2
  regress private $xlist2 income ssiratio
  predict lpuhat2, residual
  poisson docvis private $xlist2 lpuhat2
  matrix `b' = e(b)
  ereturn post `b'
end
bootstrap _b, reps(400) seed(10101) nodots nowarn: endogtwostep
=#

# `stata_boot_cf_poisson` (chunk 1) — bootstraps the whole two-step
# control-function pipeline (first-stage OLS → residual → second-stage
# Poisson) 400 times, so the SEs account for the first-stage estimation
# that §17.5.1's naive robust SEs ignore. Stata's MT64 RNG differs from
# Julia's MersenneTwister, so the bootstrap SEs match Stata only up to
# Monte Carlo noise, not digit-for-digit.
stata_boot_cf_poisson(df17_iv;
    count_dep   = :docvis,
    endog       = :private,
    exog        = [:medicaid, :age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr],
    instruments = [:income, :ssiratio],
    B = 400, seed = 10101,
    title = "Poisson two-step (control function), bootstrap SEs");
```

### 17.5.2 Nonlinear IV method

```{julia}
#=
* Nonlinear IV estimator for Poisson: computation using optimize
generate cons = 1
local y docvis
local xlist private medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr cons
local zlist income ssiratio medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr cons
* Mata code to obtain GMM estimator for Poisson using command optimize
mata 
  void pgmm(todo, b, y, X, Z, Qb, g, H)
  {
    Xb = X*b'
    mu = exp(Xb)
    h = Z'(y-mu)
    W = cholinv(cross(Z,Z))
    Qb = h'W*h
    if (todo == 0) return
    G = -(mu:*Z)'X
    g = (G'W*h)'
    if (todo == 1) return
    H = G'W*G
    _makesymmetric(H)
   }
  st_view(y=., ., "`y'")  
  st_view(X=., ., tokens("`xlist'"))
  st_view(Z=., ., tokens("`zlist'"))
  S = optimize_init()
  optimize_init_which(S,"min")
  optimize_init_evaluator(S, &pgmm())
  optimize_init_evaluatortype(S, "d2")
  optimize_init_argument(S, 1, y)
  optimize_init_argument(S, 2, X)
  optimize_init_argument(S, 3, Z)
  optimize_init_params(S, J(1,cols(X),0))
  optimize_init_technique(S,"nr")
  b = optimize(S)  
  // Compute robust estimate of VCE and SEs
  Xb = X*b'
  mu = exp(Xb)
  h = Z'(y-mu)
  W = cholinv(cross(Z,Z))
```
